intro: Python serves as a highly structured language prominent in backend services, automation, and data pipelines, while JavaScript operates as the ubiquitous event-driven engine powering modern web interfaces and network runtimes. Migrating Python code to JavaScript enables full-stack unification under a single runtime environment (such as Node.js or browsers). This switch introduces an un-opinionated asynchronous architecture driven by a single-threaded event loop and dynamic, prototype-based object linking.

diffs.from: Strong, dynamic typing. Bypasses implicit type coercion, preventing arbitrary actions like adding a string to an integer.
diffs.to: Weak, dynamic typing. Coerces variables implicitly at runtime, allowing permissive conversions using operators like + or ==.

diffs.from: Clean layouts relying on structured whitespace indentation to delineate execution blocks and function routines.
diffs.to: C-style block syntax using curly braces, explicit assignment keywords (const, let), and optional statement semicolons.

diffs.from: Automatic resource cleanup utilizing a blend of reference count registers and cyclic analysis passes.
diffs.to: Automated memory abstraction tracking variables through mark-and-sweep garbage collection algorithms built into JavaScript engines.

diffs.from: Managed explicitly using the asyncio library, wrapping synchronous workflows inside managed event engines.
diffs.to: Natively asynchronous core. Non-blocking runtime behavior is powered directly by Promises, async/await keywords, and an internal loop.

diffs.from: Broad 'batteries-included' baseline providing native tools for file management, structural protocols, and data modeling.
diffs.to: Compact core library heavily reliant on execution environments (browser DOM or Node.js APIs) and the NPM external package registry.

examples.title: Parsing and structuring JSON payloads
examples.description: Compares deserializing data objects and handling string mapping formats.
examples.fromCode:
import json
data_str = '{"id": 42, "label": "active"}'
obj = json.loads(data_str)
print(obj['label'])

examples.title: Delaying execution asynchronously
examples.description: Illustrates pausing a control block without stalling other active execution operations.
examples.fromCode:
import asyncio

async def delay_msg():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    print('Hello')

tips.title: Enforce Strict Equality Checks
tips.body: Always use the triple equals operator (===) instead of double equals (==) to avoid unexpected JavaScript implicit type coercion bugs.

tips.title: Use const by Default
tips.body: Adopt block-scoped variable declarations. Use const for all unchanging identifiers, and use let only when reassigning references.

tips.title: Understand Prototype Inheritance
tips.body: JavaScript classes are syntactic sugar over prototypes. Functions and objects inherit properties directly from active instance chains.

tips.title: Audit Array Mutability Methods
tips.body: Be aware that JavaScript array methods like sort() and reverse() modify the original array in place, mirroring Python's inline list behavior.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of Python's None in JavaScript?
faqs.answer: JavaScript has two distinct primitives: `null` represents the intentional absence of any object value, while `undefined` means a variable has been declared but not assigned.

faqs.question: How do JavaScript dictionaries differ from Python dicts?
faqs.answer: JavaScript uses standard Objects or `Map` objects. Keys in standard JavaScript objects are always coerced into strings or symbols, unlike Python dicts which accept any hashable type.

faqs.question: Can JavaScript handle parallel multi-core processing?
faqs.answer: Yes. While JavaScript runs on a single-threaded event loop, parallelism can be achieved using Worker Threads in Node.js or Web Workers in browser environments.
